wQ-GmWy_zmM.2: in a tube partially filled with water
wQ-GmWy_zmM.3: and watched as crow after crow dropped stone after stone in
wQ-GmWy_zmM.4: until the food was in beak-snatching distance.
wQ-GmWy_zmM.5: This is just one of many fascinating displays
wQ-GmWy_zmM.7: the bold, brainy family of songbirds
wQ-GmWy_zmM.8: that includes crows, ravens, jays, and magpies.
wQ-GmWy_zmM.9: To start, corvids are strategic when it comes to their food.
wQ-GmWy_zmM.10: They ransack trash bins and snatch bites
wQ-GmWy_zmM.12: They also hoard and cache their food.
wQ-GmWy_zmM.13: A single Clark's nutcracker, for instance,
wQ-GmWy_zmM.14: is thought to scatter some 30,000 pine nuts
wQ-GmWy_zmM.16: which it can recover more than nine months later.
wQ-GmWy_zmM.17: And in one experiment, scrub jays,
wQ-GmWy_zmM.19: cached and retrieved the perishable worms first
wQ-GmWy_zmM.20: and left the peanuts for later.
wQ-GmWy_zmM.21: It seemed the jays not only recalled where they buried things,
wQ-GmWy_zmM.22: but also what they buried and when.
